title: Demonstration of a Spherical Penning Trap for Single Electrons
abstract: A spherical Penning trap has well-separated, clean microwave resonances, making it attractive for precision measurements of the electron magnetic moment and for dark-photon and axion searches with trapped electrons. We demonstrate single-electron trapping in a spherical Penning trap and characterize its microwave resonance structure. The design, single-electron detection, microwave mode characterization, and advantages of this geometry are presented.
summary: The authors successfully demonstrated single-electron trapping in a novel spherical Penning trap geometry. They characterized the trap's microwave resonance spectrum, finding three distinct, measurable modes. This work validates the trap's suitability for next-generation precision measurements, such as determining the electron magnetic moment or searching for exotic particles like dark photons.
main_problem: The paper demonstrates single-electron trapping in a spherical Penning trap and characterizes its microwave resonance structure.
main_result: The measured cavity spectrum revealed three dominant resonance peaks (at ~38, 85, and 130 GHz), successfully characterizing the trap's microwave mode structure.
method: The resonance structure was characterized by sweeping the superconducting magnet field and fitting the resulting spectral peaks to a Lorentzian function.
model_or_system: The physical system is a spherical Penning trap containing a single electron, designed for high-precision measurements like electron g-factor determination or searches for dark photons/axions.
key_observables: Three dominant microwave resonance frequencies ($f_{TM\pm 11p}$), the quality factors ($Q$), and the derived cold-cavity radius ($r_0$).
important_parameters: The required relative precision for g-factor measurement ($<10^{-12}$), and the enhancement factor ($\kappa_2$) for dark-photon searches.
main_assumptions: The theoretical mode calculation assumes a perfectly conducting spherical boundary, and the analysis relies on fitting resonance peaks to Lorentzian profiles.
figures_summary: Figure 2 demonstrates single-electron detection via oscillation amplitude decay, while Figure 3 maps the cavity spectrum showing the three dominant resonance peaks.
paper_structure: The paper proceeds by detailing the trap geometry and its advantages, demonstrating single-electron trapping, characterizing the microwave resonances through field sweeps, and finally presenting the extracted mode frequencies and derived physical parameters.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics, specifically in the areas of cavity QED and precision measurements. The characterization of the mode structure and the use of the trap for fundamental constant measurements (g-factor) are core topics in the field.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The spherical trap’s geometry and (b) definition of the coordinates of the interior trap volume.

FIG. 2. (a) Counting the number of loaded electrons by moni- toring the oscillation amplitude with a parametric drive while firing the electron gun. (b) Direct measurement of γ = Nγz by exciting to a large amplitude and monitoring its decay. Different colors correspond to different numbers of trapped electrons. The inset shows the linearity of the measured γ. (c) Fourier transform of the output signal, showing a dip of a single electron.

FIG. 3. Measured microwave spectrum of the spherical trap. (a) Fast magnet-current sweep over B = 0.7–6 T (fc = 31.40– 167.99 GHz). The three dominant peaks at 38, 85, and 130 GHz are identified with TM±111, TM±11 2, and TM±11 3, respectively. The additional, weaker peaks marked with red dotted ovals correspond to the higher-order modes listed in Table II. (b)–(d) Slow re-scans of the three TM±11p resonances at ∼0.157 GHz/min, together with the Lorentzian fits (red dashed) used to extract the center frequency and quality factor quoted in each panel.

FIG. 4. (a) Calculated cyclotron damping rate γc. The typical condition to detect a quantum cyclotron transition is also shown by the red dotted line. (b) Cavity shift δωc/ωc from the measured microwave resonances, compared to the typical shift in the 2023 measurement[1]. (c) Enhancement factor κ2 for dark-photon and axion searches from the measured spherical trap’s microwave resonances.
